plainMeaning: I now check myself for the five small slips of the end-of-life fast — clinging to this life, the next life, longer living, quicker dying, or past pleasures — then beg pardon for every fault, even a mispronounced syllable, in all eighty-five, and renew my day's renunciation.
recitation: saṁleṣaṇātaṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ. ihalogā saṁsappaoge, paralogā saṁsappaoge, jiviā saṁsappaoge, maraṇā saṁsappaoge, kāmabhogā saṁsappaoge. e pāñca atiyāra māṁhe. evaṁkāre śrī samakita-mūla bāra vrata viṣe pañcyāśī atiyāra māṁhe jiko koi atiyāra, anāchāra, atikrama, vyatikrama huo hoya, tathā jāṇate, ajāṇate, sūkṣma, bādara, kāno, mātrā, miṇḍī, pada, akṣara, ochho, adhiko, haḷavo, bhārī, āgala pāchala kahyo, kahevāṇo hoya, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. desāvagāsiaṁ, uvabhogaṁ paribhogaṁ paccakkhāmi. annatthaṇābhogeṇaṁ, sahassāgāreṇaṁ, mahattarāgāreṇaṁ, savvasamāhivattiyāgāreṇaṁ, vosirāmi.

wordByWord.etymology: saṁleṣaṇā (Skt. saṁlekhanā, 'thinning-out' of body and passions) + the Old Gujarati genitive suffix -taṇā ('belonging to')
wordByWord.notes: The book here spells it with -ṣ- (saṁleṣaṇā); the standard form is saṁlekhanā.
The organizing number of every vow-block; here it counts saṁlekhanā's slips.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. aticāra → Pkt./Guj. atiyāra (book spelling with -y-)

wordByWord.notes: Carries a double sense — both 'inspect for' and 'purge' the faults.
Object of the longing named in the first atichāra.

wordByWord.gloss: in the harbouring of expectant longing (āśaṁsā-prayoga)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. (ā)saṁsā-ppaoge < Skt. āśaṁsā ('craving, hope') + prayoga ('application, employment'); locative
wordByWord.notes: Recurs identically after each of the five objects: the slip is not the wish itself but actively entertaining it during the fast. The book drops the initial ā- of āsaṁsā.

wordByWord.gloss: the next world / the life to come
in the harbouring of expectant longing (repeated)
wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence — craving a better rebirth.

wordByWord.gloss: in the harbouring of expectant longing (repeated)
wordByWord.notes: Third occurrence — wishing the fast prolong one's life.

wordByWord.gloss: in the harbouring of expectant longing (repeated)
wordByWord.notes: Fourth occurrence — impatiently craving a swift end to escape discomfort.

wordByWord.gloss: in the harbouring of expectant longing (repeated)
wordByWord.notes: Fifth occurrence — recalling or hankering after past/future pleasures.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. māṁhe ('inside, amid') < Skt. madhye
wordByWord.notes: Closes the block: 'among these five transgressions.'
Pivot from the last vow into the grand summation of the whole confession.
Doctrinally pointed: the twelve vows count for nothing without samyaktva as their root.
The book's grand total: samyaktva 5 + twelve vows 60 + fifteen karmādāna 15 + saṁlekhanā 5 = 85.
'Among these eighty-five transgressions.'

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. relative jiko / je-ko ('whatever, whichever')
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with koi to mean 'whatsoever fault of any kind.'
First of the four graded fault-degrees.
The gravest degree — a vow fully broken.
The mildest degree — merely intending or moving toward the fault.
Between atikrama and full anāchāra in severity.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. past participle of hovuṁ ('to be/become') < Skt. bhū

wordByWord.notes: The subjunctive keeps the confession all-embracing — known faults and merely possible ones alike.
First of seven contrastive pairs sweeping in every possible fault.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. kāno, name of the vertical ā-mātrā stroke
wordByWord.notes: Begins the list of pronunciation/recitation errors confessed.
A wrong or missing vowel-length.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. miṇḍī / mīṇḍuṁ, the nasal dot (anusvāra)
wordByWord.notes: A dropped or added nasalization.
Recited with too light a stress.
Recited with too heavy a stress.
'āgala pāchala' = recited out of sequence.
Active + passive together cover both deliberate and inadvertent misrecitation.
'te savi' = 'all of that' — gathers every fault just listed.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. manas, instrumental manasā → Guj. mane
wordByWord.notes: First of the threefold instrument.
Binds the three instruments to the act of fault.

wordByWord.gloss: may my wrongdoing come to nothing
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. micchā ('false, void') + mi ('to/for me') + dukkaḍaṁ < Skt. duṣkṛta ('ill-done deed')
wordByWord.notes: The sealing formula of the whole confession; here it closes the comprehensive, sutra-wide release.
Opens the closing pratyākhyāna tag that renews the tenth vow for the day.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. uvabhoga < Skt. upabhoga ('one-time enjoyment', e.g. food, drink)
Pkt. paribhoga < Skt. paribhoga ('repeated enjoyment', e.g. clothes, utensils)

wordByWord.gloss: I renounce / take the vow against
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. paccakkhāmi < Skt. pratyākhyāmi ('I formally renounce')
wordByWord.notes: The technical verb of every pratyākhyāna.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. annattha ('otherwise, except') + aṇābhoga < Skt. anābhoga ('absent-mindedness'); instrumental
wordByWord.notes: First of the four standard āgāras (exceptions) that keep the vow from breaking on an honest accident.

wordByWord.gloss: except by a sudden, involuntary act
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. sahasā ('suddenly') + āgāra < Skt. ākāra ('clause, exception'); instrumental

wordByWord.notes: When a more important duty compels the act.

wordByWord.gloss: except for whatever serves complete equanimity (e.g. a medical/spiritual emergency)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. savva ('all') + samāhi (Skt. samādhi, 'equanimity') + vattiya (Skt. pratyaya/vṛttika, 'conducing to') + āgāra; instrumental
wordByWord.notes: The broadest āgāra: an act done to preserve overall calm/health does not break the vow.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. vosirāmi < Skt. vyutsṛjāmi ('I cast off, relinquish')
wordByWord.notes: Seals the renunciation; the same verb that ends kāyotsarga and many pratyākhyānas.

literalTranslation: I examine the five transgressions of saṁlekhanā: in harbouring longing for this world, in harbouring longing for the next world, in harbouring longing for (more) life, in harbouring longing for (a quicker) death, in harbouring longing for sensual enjoyments — among these five transgressions. Thus too, regarding the venerable twelve vows rooted in right faith, among the eighty-five transgressions, whatever transgression, wrong conduct, overstep, or further overstep has occurred — also knowingly or unknowingly, subtly or grossly, by (error of) the ā-stroke, vowel-mark, anusvāra, word, or syllable, too little or too much, too light or too heavy, said or recited out of order — all of that, done by me with mind, speech, and body: may my wrongdoing come to nothing. I renounce consumables and re-usables under the deśāvagāśika (restriction); except by inadvertence, by a sudden act, by a weightier obligation, or by what serves complete equanimity — I give it up.
idiomaticTranslation: Now I search my conscience for the five subtle lapses of the last fast: clinging to this life or the next, craving to live longer or to die sooner, or hankering after the pleasures of the senses. And so, across all the eighty-five transgressions of the twelve vows that rest upon right faith, whatever fault has crept in — wrong deed, first overstep or further overstep, done knowing or unknowing, subtle or gross, even a slip of stroke or dot, syllable or word, a phrase too short or too long, too soft or too loud, recited out of turn — all of it, worked by my mind, my speech, my body: may it be undone, may it come to nothing. And I renew my vow, renouncing both what is used once and what is used again within my chosen bounds — save for what slips past me unawares, happens of a sudden, is forced by a higher duty, or is needed to keep my spirit at peace. This I let go.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This closing verse does three things in one breath. First it completes the litany by auditing saṁlekhanā, the voluntary end-of-life fast — fittingly the last item, since it is the householder's final act. Its five slips are not gross failures but failures of attitude: the fast must be undertaken in pure equanimity, so any expectant clinging (saṁsappaoge = āśaṁsā-prayoga, 'employing longing') spoils it — wanting to stay in this world, wanting a better next world, wanting the body to last, impatiently wanting it over, or replaying remembered pleasures. The previous verse closed the twelfth vow (atithi-saṁvibhāga) with the same 'pāñca atiyāra māṁhe' refrain; verse 13 reuses that exact cadence one final time, then breaks out of the per-vow pattern. With 'evaṁkāre' the reciter zooms out to the whole confession: the book's total of pañcyāśī (eighty-five) transgressions. Note 'samakita-mūla' — the vows are explicitly said to be rooted in right faith, the doctrinal anchor the preface names as preceding all twelve. The verse then deploys a remarkable safety-net: four graded fault-degrees (atikrama → vyatikrama → atiyāra → anāchāra) and seven sweeping contrastive pairs, descending even to recitation errors — a missing kāno-stroke, a dropped anusvāra (miṇḍī), a syllable too few or too many, a phrase too soft or out of order. Nothing, not even a mispronounced letter, is allowed to escape the sealing micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ, pronounced through mind, speech, and body. Finally the verse appends the deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna (desāvagāsiaṁ… vosirāmi), renewing for the coming period the tenth vow's tightening of one's permitted sphere, guarded by the four standard āgāras so an honest accident never counts as a broken vow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The five saṁlekhanā atichārs given here follow the Śvetāmbara Upāsakadaśā/Āvaśyaka formulation (ihaloga-/paraloga-/jīvia-/maraṇa-/kāmabhoga-āsaṁsā-prayoga), which differs in wording though not in spirit from the Tattvārtha-sūtra set (jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna). All express one principle: saṁlekhanā is valid only when emptied of desire — it is the thinning of the passions, not a strategy to gain anything, which is why even craving death is itself a fault. The four āgāras (anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, sarva-samādhi-pratyaya) encode the Jain distinction between intentional violation and unavoidable lapse — the same logic as jayaṇā — so that conscience is rigorous without becoming scrupulous despair.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the daily reciter this verse is the moment of total closure: having checked each vow, one now sweeps up every remaining fault — including the very mistakes made while reciting these words — and releases them. Its lesson is that thoroughness, not perfection, is asked: name the smallest slip honestly, seal it with micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ, and renew tomorrow's restraint. Even a beginner can take the four āgāras to heart — sincere effort, not flawless execution, is what the vow requires.

crossReferences.text: Upāsakadaśāḥ (Uvāsagadasāo), Ānanda's vows — the five saṁlekhanā atichārs as āśaṁsā-prayoga
Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.32 — jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna
Āvaśyaka-sūtra, Pratyākhyāna āvaśyaka — the standard āgāras (anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, savva-samāhi-vattiya)

sources.title: Sallekhana
sources.consultedFor: Definition of saṁlekhanā as the gradual thinning of body and passions, its voluntary desire-free character, and that it is a vow open to householders — confirming why expectant longing constitutes its transgression.

sources.title: सल्लेखना (Sallekhanā)
sources.consultedFor: The five aticāras of saṁlekhanā per Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.32 (jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna), cross-checked against this book's Upāsakadaśā-style set.

sources.title: Śrāvaka / Anuvrata (twelve vows and six āvaśyakas of the layperson)
sources.consultedFor: The śrāvaka's twelve vows and six āvaśyakas framework that situates the eighty-five-transgression total and the deśāvagāśika (tenth vow) renewal closing this verse.

notes: saṁsappaoge is read as (ā)saṁsā-prayoga ('employment of expectant longing'); the book drops the initial ā- found in the canonical āsaṁsappaoge. The five saṁlekhanā atichārs here are the Śvetāmbara Upāsakadaśā/Āvaśyaka set (this-world / next-world / life / death / sensual-pleasure longing) and differ in wording from the widely-cited Digambara Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.32 set, though the two are doctrinally equivalent; verified against the Wikipedia Sallekhana entries. The spelling saṁleṣaṇā (with -ṣ-) and atiyāra (with -y-) are preserved verbatim from the book per its orthography rules and not normalized. miṇḍī is the Gujarati scribal name for the anusvāra dot; kāno is the ā-vowel stroke — both glossed from standard Gujarati orthographic usage. The four āgāra meanings (anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, sarva-samādhi-pratyaya) follow the established Āvaśyaka pratyākhyāna tradition; no single citable URL was verified for the precise Anchalgachchha wording, so they are given per general Śvetāmbara usage. The bracketed Devasi/Rāi (day/night) substitution cue belongs to the sutra-level vidhi and is not part of this spoken text.
